_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Logan County, IL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.

current_courthouse.notes: The county seat of Lincoln, Illinois, is the only city named for Abraham Lincoln before his presidency — he christened it with watermelon juice in 1853. The 1905 courthouse replaced earlier structures without record loss.

previous_courthouses.fate: Logan County was formed from Sangamon County in 1839. The first courthouse was at Postville, which later became part of Lincoln. The original Postville courthouse was preserved and moved; it is now a historic site where Lincoln practiced law.
The county seat was moved to Mount Pulaski in 1848. Lincoln tried cases here on the Eighth Judicial Circuit. This building survives as the Mount Pulaski Courthouse State Historic Site.

previous_courthouses.location: Lincoln, brick courthouse on the town square
previous_courthouses.fate: The county seat moved to the new town of Lincoln in 1853, coinciding with the arrival of the Chicago and Alton Railroad. Records were transferred from Mount Pulaski. Replaced by the 1905 building.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Logan County has experienced no major courthouse fire or catastrophic record loss. Records survived two county seat relocations — from Postville to Mount Pulaski (1848) and from Mount Pulaski to Lincoln (1853). The bound record books were transferred intact each time. Logan County's documentary record is continuous from its 1839 formation from Sangamon County.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Logan County's records survive from 1839 despite two moves of the county seat — a testament to the care taken with bound record books. If {ancestor_name} bought land, married, or appeared in court in Logan County, the paper trail survives. The original Postville courthouse where Lincoln practiced law is now a museum, but the records are in the current Lincoln courthouse.
record_losses.source: Stringer, Lawrence B. History of Logan County, Illinois. 2 vols. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1911.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1839 deeds for land in the future Logan County are filed in Sangamon County records in Springfield. The Chicago and Alton Railroad's arrival in 1853 and the Illinois Central's nearby route generated substantial land transaction volume.
deeds.repository: Logan County Recorder, Lincoln; Illinois State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com IL land records

wills_estates.gaps: Early probate volume is low, reflecting the small pioneer population. Records become more substantial after 1850.
wills_estates.repository: Logan County Probate Court, Lincoln; Illinois State Archives; IRAD at UIS

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: Earliest records are sparse. Coverage improves after 1850.
marriages.repository: Logan County Clerk, Lincoln; Illinois State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good to excellent — circuit court minutes from formation, including cases from Lincoln's Eighth Judicial Circuit
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records are incomplete. Circuit court records are intact across all three courthouse locations.
court_minutes.repository: Logan County Circuit Court, Lincoln; Illinois State Archives; Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library
court_minutes.online_access: Lincoln Legal Papers project (digital); Illinois State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Illinois required county-level registration from 1877. Compliance was uneven until statewide enforcement in 1916. Lincoln city records may be more complete than rural township records.
vital_records.repository: Logan County Clerk; Illinois Department of Public Health; Illinois State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Logan County was formed from Sangamon County in 1839. Families who settled in the future Logan County before that date — including those who knew the Lincoln family — appear in Sangamon County records in Springfield.
substitute_sources.repository: Sangamon County Recorder, Springfield; Illinois State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing pioneer families before 1839. Springfield is only 30 miles from Lincoln, IL, so researchers can visit both courthouses in a single trip.
substitute_sources.source: Stringer, Lawrence B. History of Logan County, Illinois. 2 vols. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1911.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1840–1940 (first census after formation). Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) document prairie farming operations.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1850 census is especially valuable because it captures the county before the railroad's arrival; the 1860 census shows the transformation. Agricultural schedules document individual farming operations in a county that was overwhelmingly agricultural.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Logan County, IL.

substitute_sources.source_type: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Lincoln historic sites
substitute_sources.description: The Postville Courthouse State Historic Site and the Mount Pulaski Courthouse State Historic Site both preserve the actual buildings where Lincoln tried cases. The ALPL holds related manuscript collections, including court records, personal papers, and community records from Logan County's early years.
substitute_sources.repository: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library, Springfield; Postville Courthouse State Historic Site, Lincoln; Mount Pulaski Courthouse State Historic Site
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The Lincoln Legal Papers project has indexed and transcribed circuit court cases from Logan County, making these records accessible. Any family involved in litigation during Lincoln's practice years (1837–1860) may appear in these transcriptions, even if Lincoln was not their attorney.
substitute_sources.source: Lincoln Legal Papers project. The Law Practice of Abraham Lincoln: Complete Documentary Edition. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2000.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Baptist, Methodist, Christian Church (Disciples), Presbyterian
substitute_sources.description: Upper South migrants and New England-origin settlers brought diverse Protestant traditions to Logan County. Church records supplement civil records, particularly before 1877.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Logan County Genealogical and Historical Society; Illinois State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church baptism, marriage, and burial records fill gaps before 1877 civil registration. Denominational affiliation can indicate geographic origin — Cumberland Presbyterians and certain Baptist groups suggest Kentucky/Tennessee origin; Congregationalists suggest New England or upstate New York origin.
substitute_sources.source: Logan County Genealogical and Historical Society. Collections and finding aids.

narrative_hooks: Logan County's records survived two county seat moves — Postville to Mount Pulaski to Lincoln — without loss, so if {ancestor_name} left a paper trail in this county after 1839, it exists in the Lincoln courthouse today.

The city of Lincoln, Illinois, was named for Abraham Lincoln before he was president — he christened the town site with watermelon juice in 1853 — and the same circuit court records that document his law practice may document {ancestor_name}'s legal affairs.

Before 1839, what became Logan County was part of Sangamon County — if {ancestor_name} was among the earliest settlers, {pronoun_possessive} deeds, marriages, and court records are filed in Springfield, not Lincoln.

If {ancestor_name} appeared in Logan County's circuit court before 1860, the Lincoln Legal Papers project may have transcribed the case file — making it accessible online even though the original sits in an archive.

Logan County's denominational map tells a migration story: Cumberland Presbyterians came from Kentucky and Tennessee, Disciples of Christ from the Upper South, and Congregationalists from New England — {ancestor_name}'s church affiliation may be the strongest evidence of where the family came from before Illinois.
source_references: Stringer, Lawrence B. History of Logan County, Illinois. 2 vols. Chicago: Pioneer Publishing, 1911.
Lincoln Legal Papers project. The Law Practice of Abraham Lincoln: Complete Documentary Edition. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 2000.
Illinois State Archives. Descriptive Inventory of the Archives of the State of Illinois. 2nd ed. Springfield: Office of the Secretary of State, 1978.
Logan County Genealogical and Historical Society. Collections and finding aids.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Logan County, IL.
